metadata.subject: Social Studies
metadata.description: Philosophy 12 examines the fundamental questions of human existence: What can we know? What is real? What is right? How should we live? Students explore the major branches of philosophy — epistemology, metaphysics, ethics, and political philosophy — and develop skills in logical reasoning, argument analysis, and Socratic dialogue. The course challenges students to question their assumptions and engage with radically different ways of seeing the world.
metadata.general_description: An exploration of philosophy's major branches — epistemology, metaphysics, ethics, and political philosophy — developing students' capacity for logical reasoning, argument analysis, and reflective self-examination.

big_ideas.statement: Philosophy examines the fundamental nature of knowledge, reality, and existence.
big_ideas.elaboration: From ancient Greece to contemporary thought, philosophy has asked the most basic questions about the world and our place in it.

big_ideas.statement: Philosophy provides investigative tools for meaning and understanding different ways of thinking.
big_ideas.elaboration: Logic, argumentation, conceptual analysis, and dialogue are philosophical tools that help us think more clearly about any topic.

big_ideas.statement: Questioning assumptions in philosophy helps people comprehend their own beliefs.
big_ideas.elaboration: By asking why we believe what we believe, philosophy builds intellectual humility and self-awareness.

big_ideas.statement: While philosophical questions may lack definitive answers, logic and reasoned arguments can show which answers have more or less value.
big_ideas.elaboration: Philosophy does not always provide certainty, but it provides rigorous tools for evaluating competing claims and making better-informed judgments.

categories.name: Reasoning and Inquiry
categories.raw: Use philosophical inquiry processes and reasoning skills to respond to arguments and questions about major philosophical issues.

competencies.raw: Use philosophical inquiry and reasoning skills to engage with arguments and questions.
i_can_statements: I can construct a valid logical argument using deductive and inductive reasoning.
I can identify and name common logical fallacies.
I can participate in Socratic dialogue, responding to challenges with reasoned answers.
I can distinguish between a valid argument and a sound argument.

categories.name: Application
categories.raw: Assess philosophical ideas' relevance for everyday life and current social/political issues.

competencies.raw: Assess the relevance of philosophical ideas for everyday life and current social/political issues.
i_can_statements: I can apply an ethical theory (e.g., utilitarianism) to a real-world dilemma.
I can connect a philosophical concept (e.g., justice, freedom) to a contemporary political issue.
I can explain how philosophical thinking applies to decisions in my own life.

categories.name: Analysis and Assessment
categories.raw: Analyze philosophical ideas and assess their significance; compare justification for different perspectives.

competencies.raw: Analyze philosophical ideas, assess their significance, and compare justifications for different perspectives.
i_can_statements: I can analyze a philosophical text and identify its central claim and supporting arguments.
I can compare two philosophical positions on the same question and evaluate which is better justified.
I can assess the strengths and weaknesses of a philosophical argument.

categories.name: Historical Understanding
categories.raw: Compare continuity, change, and divergence across ideas, disciplines, and schools of thought; assess the development and impact of diverse ideas and systems of thought.

competencies.raw: Compare continuity, change, and divergence across philosophical ideas, disciplines, and schools of thought.
i_can_statements: I can trace how a philosophical idea (e.g., the concept of justice) has evolved from ancient to modern times.
I can explain how historical context shaped a philosopher's ideas.
I can compare philosophical traditions across cultures (e.g., Western, Indigenous, Eastern).

categories.name: Perspective
categories.raw: Explain different philosophical perspectives considering norms, values, worldviews, and beliefs of developing philosophers.

competencies.raw: Explain different philosophical perspectives considering norms, values, worldviews, and beliefs.
i_can_statements: I can explain how a philosopher's social context influenced their philosophical positions.
I can represent a philosophical position I disagree with accurately and charitably.
I can compare feminist philosophy with traditional Western philosophy on a shared question.

areas.name: Methods of Reasoning and Argument
areas.raw: Logic and rational argument, logical fallacies, Socratic dialogue, syllogisms, induction and deduction, criticizing and closing arguments, rational decision making.
topics: Logic: valid and invalid arguments
Deductive and inductive reasoning
Syllogisms: categorical, hypothetical, and disjunctive
Common logical fallacies (ad hominem, straw man, false dichotomy, etc.)
Socratic dialogue: questioning and refutation
Rational decision making: cost-benefit analysis, ethical consideration
The structure of arguments: premises, conclusions, evidence

areas.name: Metaphysical Theories
areas.raw: Idealism, realism, materialism, personal identity, determinism and free will, theism, deism, atheism, nihilism, existentialism, positivism, postmodernism.
topics: Metaphysics: the study of reality and existence
Idealism vs. realism vs. materialism
Personal identity: what makes you 'you' over time?
Determinism, free will, and compatibilism
Theism, deism, agnosticism, and atheism
Existentialism: Sartre, Camus, and the search for meaning
Nihilism: if nothing has meaning, what follows?
Postmodernism: the critique of grand narratives

areas.name: Epistemological Theories
areas.raw: Rationalism, empiricism, pragmatism, skepticism, relativism, appearance and reality.
topics: Epistemology: the study of knowledge — what we know and how
Rationalism (Descartes, Leibniz) vs. empiricism (Locke, Hume)
Pragmatism (James, Dewey): truth as what works
Skepticism: how can we be certain of anything?
Relativism vs. absolutism in knowledge
Plato's allegory of the cave: appearance vs. reality
Indigenous epistemologies: relational and holistic knowledge

areas.name: Social and Political Philosophy
areas.raw: Justice (distributive, restorative, retributive), rights (positive/negative, individual/group), contractarianism, communitarianism, libertarianism, feminist philosophy, anarchism, Marxism.
topics: Theories of justice: distributive, retributive, restorative
Rights: positive vs. negative rights; individual vs. collective rights
Social contract theory: Hobbes, Locke, Rousseau
Libertarianism: freedom, minimal government, and rights
Communitarianism: the community as the foundation of the good life
Feminist philosophy: challenging patriarchal assumptions in philosophy
Marxist philosophy: class, alienation, and ideology
Anarchism: questioning the legitimacy of the state

areas.name: Theories of Morality, Ethics, and Aesthetics
areas.raw: The good life, egoism vs. altruism, deontology, utilitarianism, virtue ethics, environmental ethics, biomedical ethics, nature and value of beauty, art and emotion, aesthetic judgment.
topics: The good life: what does it mean to live well?
Egoism vs. altruism: are we fundamentally selfish?
Deontological ethics: Kant and the categorical imperative
Utilitarianism: Mill and maximizing happiness
Virtue ethics: Aristotle and the development of character
Care ethics: Gilligan and relationships as the foundation of morality
Environmental ethics: do non-human beings have rights?
Biomedical ethics: euthanasia, abortion, genetic engineering
Aesthetics: what is beauty? What is art?
Objective vs. subjective aesthetic judgment
